frontmatter.title: 1. What is Creative AI?
frontmatter.subtitle: Setting the scene for the course

children.value: In the autumn of 2022, a sentence typed into a web form could produce a photographic-looking image of a place that does not exist. A few months later, a paragraph could be drafted by a model that had read most of the open web. By 2024 we had short videos from text, songs from text, and code from text. By 2026 these tools are everywhere — in editors, in browsers, in phones — and the question is no longer
what we should do with them
, and why is it now suddenly the subject of an undergraduate course at UiO?
Before reading further, write a one-sentence definition of
in your own words. Keep it; we will return to it at the end of week 12.
Each of the three words in
is one of the oldest contested concepts in the humanities. A common working definition is that something is creative when it is
in some context. The cognitive scientist Margaret Boden distinguishes between two senses
to the person who produced it
A child’s first drawing of a face is P-creative; the invention of perspective in early Renaissance painting is H-creative.
— putting familiar ideas together in unfamiliar ways (collage, mash-up).
— moving around inside an existing conceptual space and discovering its corners.
— changing the conceptual space itself, so that ideas previously impossible become thinkable.
We will return to this triple repeatedly. Generative AI is excellent at the first two and ambiguous about the third.
“AI” is even older as a label (the
coined the phrase in 1956) and even harder to pin down. A pragmatic working definition is:
is the study and construction of systems that perform tasks we would otherwise consider to require human cognition.

That definition is deliberately slippery: as soon as a task becomes routine (chess, OCR, spam filtering, speech-to-text), it tends to lose its “AI” status. Russell and Norvig describe four canonical framings: systems that think like humans, act like humans, think rationally, or act rationally
For our purposes, the AI in
trained on large datasets. We unpack each of those words in chapters
Putting the two together, we can define the field of study as follows:

is the use of machine-learned generative systems — for text, image, sound, video, code, 3D, or any other expressive medium — as tools, materials, or collaborators in cultural and design work. As a field of study, it asks both
what changes when they enter the practices of writing, art, music, design, journalism, and education.
Notice three things about this definition:
. We are not building yet another course on “AI for music” or “AI for writing”. The whole point of this generation of models is that the same architectures move between media.
, not only as a topic. We will use these tools, not just read about them.
both the technical and the cultural side
. Without the technical side, we cannot evaluate claims about what AI can or cannot do. Without the cultural side, we cannot evaluate what it should or should not be doing.
The history of Creative AI is older than it looks. A few landmarks help orient us.

children.alt: A simplified timeline of creative AI milestones from 1957 to today

children.value: A simplified timeline of creative AI milestones from 1957 to the mid-2020s.
Lejaren Hiller and Leonard Isaacson compose what is often cited as the first piece of music generated by a computer, using rules and pseudo-random choices.
The artist Harold Cohen develops AARON, a rule-based system that draws and later paints autonomously
are used in music composition (David Cope), in story generation (TALE-SPIN, MINSTREL), and in design.
Goodfellow and colleagues introduce a new way to train generators by pitting them against discriminators
. This kicks off the first wave of “neural” creative AI.
Google researchers turn an image classifier inside out and produce hallucinated, dog-eyed pictures that go viral. For the first time, a wide public
Vaswani et al. publish “Attention Is All You Need”
; within a few years it becomes the dominant architecture for language, image, audio, and code models.
, and large language models pass the threshold where they become useful for general writing
all land within a few months. Generative AI moves from research labs into the hands of millions of users.
Models now handle text, image, audio, and video together, run on phones, and are integrated into operating systems, browsers, and creative software.
Two patterns are worth pulling out of this list. First,
: by 2024 the same model family powers writing, drawing, coding, and speaking. Second,
public visibility lags research by years
: every “sudden” public moment (2015 DeepDream, 2022 ChatGPT) sits on top of a decade of slower academic and industrial work.
To sharpen the working definition above, here are a few things this course is
We will not survey self-driving cars, medical diagnosis, or fraud detection.
A few equations help and we will show them, but you do not need to derive backpropagation to pass the course.
Speculation about super-intelligence is a fine pub conversation but not a useful basis for a 12-week practical course in 2026.
from the humanities and social sciences on what these systems do to labour, copyright, attention, education, and the environment
of the systems, so that the criticism is grounded in experience.
In the practice session this week you will run
that you will keep all semester.
Pick one text tool and one image tool
. Make a free account if needed.
Write a single short brief (1–2 sentences) for a small creative task. For example:
“A flyer for a student concert at Chateau Neuf featuring a jazz trio.”
Use the text tool to draft the flyer text; use the image tool to draft a visual.
parameter changed (a different style, a different tone, a longer or shorter prompt). Save both versions.
One sentence on what you got, one sentence on what you expected, one sentence on the gap.
In the last 30 minutes of class, pair up and present each other’s experiments to a third student. We will then collect surprises on the board.
Throughout this course, save every prompt you write. It will become the most valuable artefact in your project portfolio at week 12.
For your weekly log, choose one prompt and write 150–300 words:
(from the start of this chapter) with the working definition we ended up with. What did you leave out? What did you include that we did not?
Pick one item from the short history above and look it up. What was the historical context (technical, cultural, economic)? Is there a similar context for the 2022 generative turn?
In your discipline, what counted as “creative work” five years ago? Which parts of it are most affected by generative AI today?
The Creative Mind: Myths and Mechanisms
(Stanford HAI) — annual snapshot of the field
Open tools you can install this week:
— open-weight chat models on your laptop
By the end of the week, can you finish this sentence in three different ways?
